preamble: The alveolar trill /r/ can undergo debuccalization or reduction, leading to its replacement by a glottal stop /ʔ/ in certain phonetic environments. This transformation often involves a loss of oral articulation and a shift to a glottal articulation, sometimes as a result of lenition or as a strategy for hiatus resolution.

examples.note: Final Proto-Kuki-Chin *r developed into a glottal stop, often via an intermediate velar stop /k/ stage.
Final Proto-Kuki-Chin *r developed into a glottal stop, often via an intermediate velar stop /k/ stage, attested in the late 19th century.
Reduction of /r/ to /ʔ/ in informal speech, for example, in 'famous for its airport' [ˈfeɪməs fəʔ ɪts ˈeəpɔːt].

sources: Frajzyngier, Z., & Shay, E. (Eds.). (2012). The Afroasiatic Languages. Cambridge University Press.
Peterson, D. A. (2017). On Kuki-Chin Subgrouping. In Sociohistorical Linguistics in Southeast Asia. Brill.
Obanijesu, J. (2026). From 'preferred' to 'peripheral'? A century-long study of linking-r in Standard Southern British English (1920s-2020s). PHONETICA.
